prompt:
A disease affects 1% of a screened population. A diagnostic test correctly flags 90% of people who truly have the disease, and wrongly flags 9% of healthy people. A randomly screened person tests positive. What is the probability they actually have the disease?

A) about 9.2% (roughly 1 in 11)
B) 90% (the test's true-positive rate)
C) about 50%
D) about 81%
E) about 1% (the underlying base rate)

Roughly 1 in 1,000 people in a low-risk group carries a particular infection. A screening test detects 99% of true carriers and produces a positive result in 1% of non-carriers. Someone from this group tests positive. How likely is it that they are truly a carrier?

A) 99% (the test's detection rate)
B) about 50%
C) about 9.0% (under 1 in 10)
D) about 91%
E) about 0.1% (the underlying base rate)

In a clinic where 30% of presenting patients have a given condition, a test correctly identifies 85% of patients who have it and falsely flags 20% of those who do not. A patient tests positive. What is the probability the patient has the condition?

A) 85% (the sensitivity)
B) about 30% (the base rate)
C) about 50%
D) about 78%
E) about 64.6% (between 60% and 65%)

A bank's fraud model reviews transactions of which 0.2% are actually fraudulent. The model catches 95% of genuine fraud and flags 3% of legitimate transactions as suspicious. A transaction is flagged. What is the probability it is actually fraudulent?

A) 95% (the model's catch rate)
B) about 6.0% (around 6%)
C) about 50%
D) about 30%
E) about 0.2% (the underlying base rate)

Across an inbox, 40% of incoming messages are spam. A filter labels 98% of true spam as spam and mislabels 5% of legitimate mail as spam. A message gets labeled spam. What is the probability it really is spam?

A) 98% (the filter's true-positive rate)
B) about 40% (the base rate)
C) about 80%
D) about 92.9% (between 92% and 94%)
E) about 67%

An intrusion-detection system monitors sessions of which only 0.05% are genuine attacks. It detects 99% of real attacks and raises an alert on 2% of benign sessions. An alert fires. What is the probability this session is a genuine attack?

A) about 2.4% (well under 3%)
B) 99% (the detection rate)
C) about 50%
D) about 25%
E) about 0.05% (the underlying base rate)

On a production line, 5% of units are defective. An automated inspector correctly rejects 92% of defective units and wrongly rejects 4% of good units. A unit is rejected. What is the probability it is truly defective?

A) 92% (the inspector's true-positive rate)
B) about 5% (the base rate)
C) about 54.8% (between 53% and 56%)
D) about 75%
E) about 88%

Historically, 10% of tested variants genuinely beat the control. An analytics pipeline produces a "significant win" signal for 80% of variants that truly win, but also for 5% of variants that do not. A variant gets a "win" signal. What is the probability it is a genuine winner?

A) 80% (the true-detection rate)
B) about 10% (the base rate)
C) about 40%
D) about 75%
E) about 64% (around 64%)

In a workforce, 4% of employees use a banned substance. A drug screen returns positive for 97% of users and for 5% of non-users. An employee tests positive. What is the probability they actually used the substance?

A) 97% (the test's sensitivity)
B) about 44.7% (around 45%)
C) about 4% (the base rate)
D) about 65%
E) about 90%

Among loan applicants, 8% will default. A risk model flags 88% of future defaulters and 10% of applicants who will repay. An applicant is flagged high-risk. What is the probability they will actually default?

A) 88% (the model's true-positive rate)
B) about 8% (the base rate)
C) about 70%
D) about 43.3% (between 42% and 45%)
E) about 90%

A cancer affects 2% of a screened cohort. The screen detects 93% of true cases and yields a positive result for 7% of cancer-free people. A patient screens positive. What is the probability they have the cancer?

A) about 21.3% (around 21%)
B) 93% (the screen's detection rate)
C) about 50%
D) about 70%
E) about 2% (the base rate)

Of links employees click, 15% lead to phishing pages. A classifier flags 96% of true phishing links and 8% of safe links. A link is flagged. What is the probability it is genuinely phishing?

A) 96% (the classifier's true-positive rate)
B) about 15% (the base rate)
C) about 67.9% (between 67% and 70%)
D) about 85%
E) about 88%

A genetic variant occurs in 0.5% of people. A lab assay correctly identifies 98% of carriers and gives a false positive in 3% of non-carriers. A sample tests positive. What is the probability the person carries the variant?

A) 98% (the assay's sensitivity)
B) about 50%
C) about 33%
D) about 0.5% (the base rate)
E) about 14.1% (around 14%)

One percent of batches are contaminated. A purity test flags 99% of contaminated batches and 2% of clean batches. A batch is flagged. What is the probability it is actually contaminated?

A) 99% (the test's true-positive rate)
B) about 33.3% (around 1 in 3)
C) about 50%
D) about 66%
E) about 1% (the base rate)

On a platform, 20% of new signups are bots. A detector flags 90% of bots and 10% of real users. A signup is flagged as a bot. What is the probability it really is a bot?

A) 90% (the detector's true-positive rate)
B) about 20% (the base rate)
C) about 50%
D) about 69.2% (between 68% and 70%)
E) about 82%

In a population, 1 in 10,000 individuals is on a target list. A facial-recognition match correctly identifies 99% of true targets and falsely matches 1% of everyone else. The system reports a match. What is the probability the matched person is truly a target?

A) about 0.98% (about 1%)
B) 99% (the match accuracy)
C) about 50%
D) about 10%
E) about 0.01% (the base rate)

Three percent of machines will fail within a week. A predictive-maintenance model flags 85% of machines that will fail and 6% of machines that will not. A machine is flagged. What is the probability it will actually fail within the week?

A) 85% (the model's true-positive rate)
B) about 3% (the base rate)
C) about 30.5% (around 30%)
D) about 60%
E) about 80%

Six percent of competing athletes are doping. An anti-doping test returns positive for 94% of dopers and 2% of clean athletes. An athlete tests positive. What is the probability the athlete is actually doping?

A) 94% (the test's sensitivity)
B) about 6% (the base rate)
C) about 50%
D) about 90%
E) about 75% (around 75%)

Half a percent of bills passing through a machine are counterfeit. The machine flags 92% of counterfeits and 1% of genuine bills. A bill is flagged. What is the probability it is counterfeit?

A) 92% (the flag rate for counterfeits)
B) about 31.6% (around 32%)
C) about 50%
D) about 70%
E) about 0.5% (the base rate)

In a flagged review queue, 35% of reported emails are truly malicious. A secondary scanner confirms 90% of truly malicious emails and confirms 15% of benign ones. The scanner confirms an email as malicious. What is the probability it really is malicious?

A) 90% (the scanner's true-positive rate)
B) about 35% (the base rate)
C) about 60%
D) about 76.4% (between 76% and 78%)
E) about 85%

A rare adverse reaction occurs in 0.2% of patients on a drug. A monitoring algorithm flags 80% of true reactions and 5% of patients with unrelated symptoms. A patient is flagged. What is the probability they had the true adverse reaction?

A) about 3.1% (around 3%)
B) 80% (the algorithm's detection rate)
C) about 50%
D) about 16%
E) about 0.2% (the base rate)

One percent of large trades involve insider information. A surveillance system flags 85% of true insider trades and 4% of legitimate large trades. A trade is flagged. What is the probability it involved insider information?

A) 85% (the system's true-positive rate)
B) about 50%
C) about 17.7% (around 18%)
D) about 1% (the base rate)
E) about 30%

Ten percent of chips have a latent defect. A burn-in test catches 95% of defective chips and fails 7% of good chips. A chip fails burn-in. What is the probability it is genuinely defective?

A) 95% (the test's true-positive rate)
B) about 10% (the base rate)
C) about 40%
D) about 88%
E) about 60.1% (around 60%)

On any given monitored day, there is a 2% chance of an actual wildfire-smoke event in a region. A sensor network alerts on 97% of real events and on 10% of non-event days. An alert fires. What is the probability there is a genuine smoke event?

A) 97% (the sensor's true-positive rate)
B) about 16.5% (around 17%)
C) about 50%
D) about 2% (the base rate)
E) about 30%

One in 1,000 login attempts is an account takeover. A risk engine flags 90% of takeovers and 0.5% of legitimate logins. A login is flagged. What is the probability it is a genuine takeover?

A) 90% (the engine's true-positive rate)
B) about 50%
C) about 0.1% (the base rate)
D) about 15.3% (around 15%)
E) about 30%

Five percent of online-exam sessions involve cheating. A proctoring model flags 88% of cheating sessions and 3% of honest sessions. A session is flagged. What is the probability cheating actually occurred?

A) about 60.7% (around 61%)
B) 88% (the model's true-positive rate)
C) about 5% (the base rate)
D) about 30%
E) about 80%

Among people taking a test, 25% truly have the target hormone present. The test is positive for 99% of those who do and 2% of those who do not. A test is positive. What is the probability the hormone is truly present?

A) 99% (the test's sensitivity)
B) about 25% (the base rate)
C) about 94.3% (between 93% and 95%)
D) about 75%
E) about 88%

Roughly 0.8% of traffic spikes are genuine DDoS attacks. A monitor flags 96% of real attacks and 5% of benign spikes. A spike is flagged. What is the probability it is a genuine attack?

A) 96% (the monitor's true-positive rate)
B) about 50%
C) about 0.8% (the base rate)
D) about 30%
E) about 13.4% (around 13%)

Across inspected components, 0.3% have a critical structural defect. A scanner detects 90% of true defects and flags 2% of sound components. A component is flagged. What is the probability it has a critical defect?

A) 90% (the scanner's detection rate)
B) about 11.9% (around 12%)
C) about 50%
D) about 0.3% (the base rate)
E) about 25%

About 1.5% of orders result in chargeback fraud. A model flags 92% of fraudulent orders and 6% of honest ones. An order is flagged. What is the probability it is fraudulent?

A) 92% (the model's true-positive rate)
B) about 50%
C) about 1.5% (the base rate)
D) about 18.9% (around 19%)
E) about 30%

Thirty percent of subscribers will churn next quarter. A churn model flags 78% of true churners and 18% of those who will stay. A subscriber is flagged. What is the probability they will actually churn?

A) about 65% (around 65%)
B) 78% (the model's true-positive rate)
C) about 30% (the base rate)
D) about 50%
E) about 72%

Twelve percent of patients tested truly have a specific allergy. A skin test is positive for 85% of allergic patients and 10% of non-allergic ones. A patient tests positive. What is the probability they are truly allergic?

A) 85% (the test's true-positive rate)
B) about 12% (the base rate)
C) about 53.7% (around 54%)
D) about 70%
E) about 80%

Around 0.4% of monitored accounts are involved in money laundering. A transaction-monitoring system alerts on 93% of laundering accounts and 3% of clean accounts. An account triggers an alert. What is the probability it is genuinely involved in laundering?

A) 93% (the system's true-positive rate)
B) about 50%
C) about 0.4% (the base rate)
D) about 30%
E) about 11.1% (around 11%)

About 0.7% of servers have an imminent disk fault. A telemetry model flags 88% of failing disks and 1.5% of healthy ones. A server is flagged. What is the probability its disk is truly failing?

A) 88% (the model's true-positive rate)
B) about 29.3% (around 29%)
C) about 50%
D) about 0.7% (the base rate)
E) about 15%

On a forum, 45% of submitted comments are spam. A moderation model flags 92% of spam comments and 12% of legitimate comments. A comment is flagged. What is the probability it really is spam?

A) 92% (the model's true-positive rate)
B) about 45% (the base rate)
C) about 65%
D) about 86.3% (between 86% and 88%)
E) about 90%

Before any testing, the chance a patient has a condition is 1%. A biomarker is present in 95% of patients with the condition and in 10% of patients without it. The biomarker is present. What is the updated probability the patient has the condition?

A) about 8.8% (around 9%)
B) 95% (the biomarker's true-positive rate)
C) about 50%
D) about 1% (the base rate)
E) about 30%

2.5% of pump units have a bearing defect. A vibration analysis flags 90% of defective pumps and 5% of healthy pumps. A pump is flagged. What is the probability it truly has a bearing defect?

A) 90% (the analysis's true-positive rate)
B) about 2.5% (the base rate)
C) about 31.6% (around 32%)
D) about 60%
E) about 85%

Two percent of merged commits introduce a real regression. A flaky CI heuristic flags 80% of true regressions and 3% of clean commits. A commit is flagged. What is the probability it introduced a real regression?

A) 80% (the heuristic's true-positive rate)
B) about 2% (the base rate)
C) about 50%
D) about 77%
E) about 35.2% (around 35%)

A disease has a prevalence of 1 in 1,000. A high-specificity test detects 95% of true cases and gives a false positive in only 0.1% of healthy people. A person tests positive. What is the probability they have the disease?

A) 95% (the test's sensitivity)
B) about 48.7% (around 49%)
C) about 5%
D) about 99%
E) about 0.1% (the base rate)

Ten percent of insurance claims are fraudulent. An adjuster's model flags 70% of fraudulent claims and 10% of legitimate ones. A claim is flagged. What is the probability it is fraudulent?

A) 70% (the model's true-positive rate)
B) about 10% (the base rate)
C) about 60%
D) about 43.7% (around 44%)
E) about 65%
